coredata.dc:title: PEI N-doped graphene quantum dots/p-type silicon Schottky diode
coredata.dc:creator: Berktaş, Zeynep
Yıldız, Mustafa
Seven, Elanur
Oz Orhan, Elif
Altındal, Şemsettin
coredata.dc:description: Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are graphene nanoparticles with exceptional properties and a remarkable material for new technologies. Especially, GQDs/semiconductor junctions (GQDs/S), which have a high potential to open doors to new fields of study such as sensing, detection, and communication have started to attract great attention in the scientific community in the last few years. Functionalization of GQDs with several functional groups to develop their properties is one of the hot topics of research. In this context, we have investigated the properties of functionalized GQDs/p-type Si diode in this study. Polyethylenimine (PEI) functionalized nitrogen-doped GQDs (PEI/N/GQDs) have been successfully produced by the hydrothermal method. Afterward, we fabricated the Al/PEI/N/GQDs/p-Si diode. The parameters of GQDs-based Schottky diode have been investigated from current-voltage (I-V) measurements at 300 K. The barrier heights (Φb) of the diode obtained from Thermionic Emission (TE) theory, Norde’s, and Cheung’s approaches are 0.76 eV, 0.82 eV, and 0.66 eV, respectively. The ratio of rectification (RR) of the diode has been calculated to be approximately 2.8 × 104 at ± 5 V. In addition, the surface state density (Nss) versus (Ess-Ev) profile of the diode has been also obtained, by taking into account the voltage dependence of the ideality factor (n) and Φb. The average value of them has been found as 1.90 × 1013 eV−1cm−2, which is very suitable for this structure. The obtained results show that the prepared Al/PEI/N/GQDs/p-Si has Schottky diode behavior.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs)
PEI functionalized N-doped GQDs
Schottky diode
I-V characteristics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of PEI/N/GQDs (FT-IR, UV–vis, TEM, and EDX)
Electrical properties of Al/ PEI/N/GQDs /p Si structure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic representation of Al/PEI/N/GQDs/p-Si structure.
The FT-IR spectra of the PEI/N/GQDs.
Absorption spectrum of PEI/N/GQDs in aqueous suspensions.
Ln(I)-V plot of the Al/ PEI/N/GQDs /p Si.
A schematic energy band diagram of Al/PEI/N/GQDs/p-Si structure.
The dV/d(lnI) versus I and H(I) versus I characteristics of Al/ PEI/N/GQDs /p- Si.
F(V, I) from the Norde’s plot of the Al/ PEI/N/GQDs /p Si.
The energy dependent profile of Nss of the Al/ PEI/N/GQDs /p Si.
Electrical parameters of the Al/ PEI/N/GQDs /p Si structure at room temperature.

head.ce:title: PEI N-doped graphene quantum dots/p-type silicon Schottky diode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Gazi University, Department of Advanced Technologies, 06500 Ankara, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Gazi University, Department of Advanced Technologies, 06500 Ankara, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Çanakkale Onsekiz Mart University, Department of Chemistry, 17100 Çanakkale, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Çanakkale Onsekiz Mart University, Department of Chemistry, 17100 Çanakkale, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yüksek İhtisas University, Electroneurophysiology Program, 06800 Ankara, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yüksek İhtisas University, Electroneurophysiology Program, 06800 Ankara, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Gazi University, Department of Physics, 06500 Ankara, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Gazi University, Department of Physics, 06500 Ankara, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are graphene nanoparticles with exceptional properties and a remarkable material for new technologies. Especially, GQDs/semiconductor junctions (GQDs/S), which have a high potential to open doors to new fields of study such as sensing, detection, and communication have started to attract great attention in the scientific community in the last few years. Functionalization of GQDs with several functional groups to develop their properties is one of the hot topics of research. In this context, we have investigated the properties of functionalized GQDs/p-type Si diode in this study. Polyethylenimine (PEI) functionalized nitrogen-doped GQDs (PEI/N/GQDs) have been successfully produced by the hydrothermal method. Afterward, we fabricated the Al/PEI/N/GQDs/p-Si diode. The parameters of GQDs-based Schottky diode have been investigated from current-voltage (I-V) measurements at 300 K. The barrier heights (Φb) of the diode obtained from Thermionic Emission (TE) theory, Norde’s, and Cheung’s approaches are 0.76 eV, 0.82 eV, and 0.66 eV, respectively. The ratio of rectification (RR) of the diode has been calculated to be approximately 2.8 × 104 at ± 5 V. In addition, the surface state density (Nss) versus (Ess-Ev) profile of the diode has been also obtained, by taking into account the voltage dependence of the ideality factor (n) and Φb. The average value of them has been found as 1.90 × 1013 eV−1cm−2, which is very suitable for this structure. The obtained results show that the prepared Al/PEI/N/GQDs/p-Si has Schottky diode behavior.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are nanocrystalline semiconductor materials with unique properties whose optical and electronic properties are determined by the shape and size of the dots. These particles can vary within about 2–15 nm in the order of 10–75 atomic lengths in diameter. The small size of the particle gives it a high surface-to-volume ratio, so its properties have a very important place between individual molecules and bulk semiconductors [1–4]. QDs are obtained from the III-V and II-VI group elements of the periodic table [5]. It is possible to obtain QDs from almost all semiconductor metal compounds. By changing the size of the quantum dots, the band gap can also be changed. The change in the size of the quantum dots with the effect of quantum restriction also changes the color of the radiation they make [6–8]. It is a controllable process to make quantum dots radiate at all visible frequencies and even emit infrared radiation. Thence, quantum dots promise great advances for LEDs, electronics, solar panels, computer applications, and medical imaging processes [5,9–13].

Although inorganic semiconductor QDs are attractive materials in the last two decades, they have had some problems [14]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can better manage the problems. They demonstrate many soluble in diverse solvents, good photochemical stability, low toxicity, heteroatom-doping, and functional groups that can be added to the edges [15]. GQDs are less than 30 nm in size and a few atoms thick. GQDs are superior materials due to their large diameter, high surface area, conjugated π-π bond, high mechanical strength, high elasticity, thermal stability, and good electrical conductivity like graphene [16]. GQDs obtained from single or few sheets of graphene fragments have GQDs are also semiconductors. As a zero-dimensional material, GQDs has been studied in photodetection [17–19], light-emitting diode [20,21], photovoltaics [22,23], biosensing [24,25], bioimaging [26–29] and many similar areas [30,31]. Doping of GQDs with heteroatoms can improve their properties for the performance of the electronic device, and energy conversion/storage [32]. Nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-doped GQDs) hold promise in modulating their chemical, electronic and structural functionality, as well as further enhancing the superior properties of GQDs in energy and environmental applications. Also, N-doped GQDs can be further optimized by functionalization for particular applications. Many studies have been conducted on the synthesis and applications of N-doped GQDs [32–37]. The synthesis, characterization, and applications of nitrogen-doped graphene grown by chemical vapor deposition were investigated [38]. An investigation of N-doped GQDs by laser ablation in liquid solvents for photoluminescence and electrocatalytic applications [39]. GQDs-based Schottky structures, which are very important in electronics technology, have begun to be investigated in the last few years and efforts are being made to develop devices with very fast switching capabilities that can operate at low forward voltages. Majumder et al [40] reported that GQDs' band alignment occurred in effective charge carrier transport under the illumination of light and increased the photo-sensing behaviors of ZnO. Saurob et al [41] reported that GQDs-based ZnO-nanorods-poly(3,4‑ethylene dioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT: PSS) Schottky junction was fabricated and that the device showed superior external-quantum-efficiency (EQE) at all bias voltage. Functionalized GQDs are widely used to improve their properties. GQDs can be functionalized with carboxyl, amide, nitro, aldehyde, cyano, and isopropyl groups. Nanocomposites of GQDs functionalized with PEI, which is an amino-group polymer, were synthesized for medical and biological applications [42,43]. Nag et al [21] reported that films of N-doped GQDs on silicon (SiO2) showed interesting conduction properties. It was concluded that doping nitrogen made these samples a good nominee in semiconductor applications because of enriching the electrical transport properties of these samples. PEI-functionalized N-doped GQDs structure can be modified diode and interface properties of the Schottky junction.

In this work, we investigate the diode parameters and the interface properties of PEI/N/GQDs/p Si structure from its electrical properties by measuring I-V properties, which have not been reported hitherto.

To create the diode structure, firstly, PEI/N/GQDs were successfully synthesized by a hydrothermal process. The PEI (Mw: 1800, 50 wt% in H2O) and citric acid (CA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The detail of the synthesis of PEI/N/GQDs is the same as reported in the previous work [42]. By the hydrothermal reaction of CA and PEI, PEI/N/GQDs have been synthesized in an autoclave aqueous medium [42]. The synthesis of PEI/N/GQDs was given in Fig. 1
. The characterization of PEI/N/GQDs was performed by using some characterization methods.

In this study, p-type Si (100) was used as a substrate, and Aluminum (Al) (with a purity of 99.999 %) was preferred for Schottky and ohmic contacts. First, the p-type Si (100) wafer was cleaned by applying the chemical cleaning procedures explained in detail previously [44,45]. Afterward, Al (purity of 99.999 %) was deposited by using the physical vapor deposition technique (PVD) with a thickness of 124 nm to obtain ohmic contacts. The coated wafer was annealed at 500° C for 10 min in a vacuum for bonding Al to the unpolished surface of the wafer. Second, The PEI/N/GQDs solutions were spin-coated on the p-type Si wafer. Finally, the 128 nm thick Al metal (99.99 %) rectifying contacts with using a diameter of 1 mm metal shadow mask were deposited by using PVD on the GQDs/p Si structure. All evaporation operations were performed at approximately 2.7x10−7 kPa. Fig. 2 shows the schematic representation of Al/ PEI/N/GQDs /p Si structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of PEI/N/GQDs (FT-IR, UV–vis, TEM, and EDX)
ce:section.ce:para: The PEI/N/GQDs’ FT-IR (Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy) spectra show variations in functional group frequencies (Fig. 3 ). CO, CC, CN, CO, CH, OH + NH2 + NH + COOH vibration bands are observed at 1304, 1448, 1561, 1642, 3490–3335-3212, 2974–2841 cm−1 respectively. OH + NH2 + NH + COOH bands overlap in the PEI/N/GQDs spectrum. Fig. 4 shows the steady-state absorption spectrum of PEI/N/GQDs in aqueous suspensions. The PEI/N/GQDs’ UV–vis (Ultraviolet–Visible) spectra exhibit two bands at 240 nm and 363 nm which are assigned to the π–π* and n–π* transition of CC, and CN and CO, respectively. Karatay et al [42]. show that the fluorescence emission spectrum of PEI N-doped GQDs (λem = 446 nm) overlaps partially with their absorption spectra. Thus, it can be supposed that an energy transfer process takes place in the PEI N-doped GQDs in which GQDs act as the donor. The PEI/N/GQDs’ morphology and structure were also confirmed by its TEM (Transmission Electron Microscopy) image as shown in Fig. 5 . The TEM analysis verifies the presence of the PEI/N/GQDs (particle sizes; 10–17 nm). Also, the additional characterization was performed SEM (Scanning Electron Microscopy) with the EDX (Energy Dispersive X-ray Analysis) detector. Fig. 6 shows the amounts of elements detected by using the EDX method; nitrogen, carbon, and oxygen were observed on the surface of the samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical properties of Al/ PEI/N/GQDs /p Si structure
ce:section.ce:para:
Al/ PEI/N/GQDs /p Si structure was investigated using the TE theory to discover the electrical properties. The forwardI–Vcharacteristics of the diode obeying TE theory can be described by the following relation [46–51].
(1)
I =
I 0
e x p
q
V - I
R s
nkT
wheren,IRs, V, q, k, T, and I0 are the ideality factor, the voltage drop on series resistance(Rs), the applied bias voltage, the electronic charge, Boltzmann’s constant, the absolute temperature in Kelvin, reverse saturation current respectively. The reverse saturation current of the Schottky diode can be written as.
(2)
I 0
= A
A
∗
T
2
e x p
-
q
ϕ b
kT
where k is Boltzmann's constant, T is the temperature in Kelvin (K) and q is the electron charge, and Φb is the effective barrier height. Also, A is the device area, A* is defined by the Richardson constant value as 32 A/cm2K2 for p Si. The Φb value is obtained from Eq. (2). The n is calculated from the slope of semi-logarithmic I-V plots and is given as [46–51].
(3)
n =
q
kT
dV
d
I n I

Al/ PEI/N/GQDs /p Si structure’s Ln(I)-V plot is represented in Fig. 7
. The structure represents good rectifying behavior. The rectifying ratio (RR) was obtained to be about 2.8.104 at ± 5 V. As can be seen from Fig. 7, the semi-logarithmic I-V plot of the Al/PEI/N/GQDs/p-Si structure has a good linear region in the intermediate forward bias voltages and then deviated from the linearity due to the effects of Rs and interfacial layer. Because the applied bias voltage across the diode will be shared between the depletion layer, Rs, and interfacial layer. Moreover, the structure has good rectification properties (RR = Iforw./Irev. = 2.8x104 at ± 5 V) and the leakage current is extremely low. All these results show that the prepared Al/(PEI/N/GQDs)/p-Si has Schottky diode behavior. A schematic energy band diagram of Al/PEI/N/GQDs/p-Si structure was given in Fig. 8 .

For that reason, Rs is determined by Norde’s approach [52] and Cheung's [53] functions. Moreover, the n and Φb values are calculated by the Cheung's method. According to this method, dV/dln(I) and H(I) can be given as,
(4)
dV
dln ( I )
= I
R s
+
nkT
q
(5)
H
I
= V -
nkT

I n
I
A

∗

2
= I
R s
+ n
ϕ b

dV/dln(I) vs I and H(I) vs I plots experimentally generated graphics are revealed for the Al/ PEI/N/GQDs /p Si structure as seen in Fig. 9 . Rs is evaluated from the slope of dV/dln(I) vs I plot and also, and nkT/q is evaluated from the y-axis interception point of dV/dln(I) vs I plot. The value of n is found from the cut point of the curve. In Eq. (5), Rs from the slope of H(I) vs I curve and Φb the cut-off point of the curve is obtained using the n value found in Eq. (4).

In addition, Φb and Rs values are calculated by Norde’s function technique [52]. This equation can be explained as follows,
(6)
F
V , I
=
V γ
-
kT
q
ln
I
A
A
∗
T
2
where γ is an integer higher than the obtained n value. Φb is described as follows.
(7)
∅ b
= F
V
min
+
V
min
γ
-
kT
q

Rs is computed as follows,
(8)
R s
=
kT ( γ - n )
q
I
min
where F(Vmin) is the minimum point of the F(V, I)-V plot, and Imin is the current corresponding to the Vmin value. F(V, I)-V plot of the structure is shown in Fig. 10
.

In Norde’s method, the F-V graph has a minimum at low voltages and the RS value is calculated from this minimum. But the Cheung function is valid at enough high forward bias voltage which is corresponding to the deviation from linearity in the LnI vs V plot and so is closer to the real Rs value of the structure. The value of Ri for high forward bias voltage and enough low reverse bias voltage becomes almost constant and these values are corresponding to the real value of Rs and shunt-resistance (Rsh), respectively. The values of Rs and Rshwere calculated from the simple Ohm's law (Ri = dVi/dIi). The value of Ri becomes almost constant at about ± 5 V, which are corresponding to the real value of Rs and Rsh as 400 Ω and 11.2 MΩ. The value of Ri is a strong function of applied bias voltage.

Al/ PEI/N/GQDs /p Si structure was characterized by using TE, Norde, and Cheung approaches. Table 1 presented the calculated n, Φb, and Rs values. The Φb values were 0.76 eV (TE theory), 0.82 eV (Norde approach), and 0.66 eV (Cheung approach). Moreover, the value of n is evaluated at about 3.71 and 9.20 according to the TE theory and Cheung’s approaches, respectively. The n values larger than unity are attributed to the barrier being inhomogeneous, interface impurities, series resistance, and interface states [54–57]. In addition, the n values obtained from various methods are different, because it is calculated as a result of using different zones of the I-V graph. Then, the values of Rs are found at 5450 Ω from Norde’s functions and 143 Ω and 110 Ω from the Cheung functions. The Rs values extracted from Cheung's method are smaller than those of Norde’s approach because Cheung’s calculation process is carried out for a nonlinear high-voltage region of the forward bias of I-V plots while Norde’s process is executed for the all forward bias region of I-V plots.

The energy-dependent profile of Nss can be extracted by using Card-Rhoderick [46], method from the I-V data at the forward bias region by considering voltage-dependent n and Φb by using the following equations [46],
(9)
n
V
= q V / [ k T ln
I
I 0
(10)
∅ e
=
∅
BO
+
1 -
1
n
V
V

In addition, the energy position of Nss for a p-type semiconductor is given as follows [46]:
(11)
E
ss
-
E v
= q
(
∅ e
- V )

Thus, the Nss vs (Ess-Ev) plot was extracted from Eqs. (9), (10), and (11) as follows [46]:
(12)
N
ss
V
=
1 q
[
ε i
d
( n
V
- 1 )
-
ε s
W d

In Eq.12, Wd is the depletion layer width and it is extracted from the capacitance–voltage (C-V) measurement as 5.72x10-6 cm for 500 kHz and represented in Fig. 11 . As can be seen in Fig. 9, the value of Nss is an exponential growth from the mid-gap of Si toward the top of the valance band edge. Their value of them altered from 3.33x1013 eV-1cm−2 at 0.376-Ess to 5.61x1012 eV-1cm−2 at 0.713-Ess at room temperature which is reasonable for this structure. The changes in Nss with energy were attributed to a special density distribution of them between metal and semiconductor.

In this study, we investigated the properties of GQDs/p-type Si diode, which can open the door to new study fields such as sensing, detection, and communication. By using the hydrothermal method, PEI/N/GQDs were successfully synthesized with CA and PEI. The I-V profile of the Al/ PEI/N/GQDs /p Si structure was examined for three commonly used approaches in this area. In addition, the Nss versus (Ess-Ev) profile of the Al/ PEI/N/GQDs /p Si structure was extracted, taking into account the dependent on the voltage of n and Φb. The obtained results show that the prepared Al/(PEI/N/GQDs)/p-Si has Schottky diode behavior.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
